subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look down at the ground beneath your feet. Whether you are standing on the sandy shore of the Indian Ocean near Lamu, on the dark volcanic soil of the highlands, or on the dusty semi-arid land near Garissa, you are standing on rock. Rock is the solid material that makes up the outer layer of our whole planet. Some of it is buried deep; some of it pokes out as mountains, cliffs and boulders; and some of it has been ground down into the sand and soil that feeds every farm. In this unit you will learn what rocks are, the three great families they belong to, how they form, and how they are slowly and endlessly recycled in a grand journey called the rock cycle.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Explain the difference between a rock and a mineral, with everyday examples.
Describe the three main types of rock - igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic - and how each one forms.
Explain how weathering breaks rock down and how erosion carries the pieces away.
Describe how loose pieces (sediment) are transported by rivers and settle into layers that harden into sedimentary rock.
Explain how fossils form and why they are almost always found in sedimentary rock.
Describe the rock cycle and explain how one type of rock can slowly turn into another.
Explain how soil forms from weathered rock and why healthy soil matters for farming.
Give examples of how people use rocks and minerals in building, farming, trade and daily life.
Carry out simple tests, such as a scratch-hardness test, and interpret the results.

concepts.title: Rocks and Minerals: What Is the Difference?
concepts.explanation: Before we can talk about the three types of rock, we must be clear about two words that people often mix up: rock and mineral. They are not the same thing, and knowing the difference is the first step to thinking like a geologist. A mineral is a natural solid substance with a fixed chemical make-up. Each mineral is always built from the same ingredients arranged in the same way, so it has the same properties wherever you find it. Salt is a mineral - it is always made of the same particles, whether it comes from a…
concepts.example: A rock is a natural solid that is usually made of a mixture of minerals stuck together. Think of it this way: if a mineral is a single ingredient, a rock is the finished dish made from several ingredients. Granite, for…

concepts.title: Igneous Rock: Born from Fire
concepts.explanation: The first great family of rock is igneous rock. The word 'igneous' comes from an old word for fire, and that is a perfect name, because igneous rock is born from heat. Deep inside the Earth it is so hot that rock is molten - a thick, glowing liquid called magma. When this molten rock cools down and hardens, it becomes solid igneous rock. There are two closely related words to learn. While the molten rock is still beneath the surface, we call it magma. When a volcano erupts and the molten rock pours out onto the…
concepts.example: Here is a beautiful piece of science. The speed at which the molten rock cools decides what the igneous rock looks like, especially the size of its crystals.

concepts.title: Sedimentary Rock: Built in Layers
concepts.explanation: The second great family is sedimentary rock. If igneous rock is born from fire, sedimentary rock is built slowly, layer by layer, from broken pieces of older rock and the remains of living things. Sedimentary rock is where we find almost all fossils, and it is the rock most useful for building. Understanding how it forms means understanding a chain of four steps. Step 1 - Weathering breaks rock into pieces
concepts.example: It all begins when older rock is broken into small loose pieces by weathering. Weathering is the wearing down of rock where it stands, by the weather and by living things. In the strong sun of the dry season a rock…

concepts.title: Metamorphic Rock: Changed by Heat and Pressure
concepts.explanation: The third great family is metamorphic rock. The word 'metamorphic' means 'changed in form', and that is exactly what these rocks are: older rocks that have been changed into something new by enormous heat and pressure, deep inside the Earth - but without melting completely. If they melted fully, they would become magma and later igneous rock. Metamorphic rock is what you get when a rock is cooked and squeezed almost to melting, so that its minerals rearrange into a new, usually harder form. Two forces do the work.…
concepts.example: The best way to understand metamorphic rock is through before-and-after pairs:

concepts.title: The Rock Cycle: Nature's Great Recycling
concepts.explanation: Now comes the idea that ties the whole unit together: the rock cycle. So far you have met three families of rock as if they were separate. But they are not separate at all. Over immense stretches of time, any type of rock can be changed into any other type. Rock is constantly being destroyed and rebuilt in a never-ending loop. This loop is the rock cycle, and it is one of the grandest ideas in all of Earth science. Let us follow a single piece of rock around the cycle. Imagine it starts as black basalt, an igneous…
concepts.example: Weathering and erosion attack the basalt. Sun, rain and roots break it into small pieces of sediment, and a river carries the pieces away.

concepts.title: Weathering and Erosion in Detail
concepts.explanation: You have already met weathering and erosion as the first steps in making sedimentary rock. They are so important that they deserve a closer look. The two words are often confused, so hold on to this simple rule: weathering breaks rock; erosion carries it away. Weathering happens on the spot; erosion is the transport. Physical weathering (also called mechanical weathering) breaks rock into smaller pieces without changing what it is made of. The pieces are still the same rock, just smaller. In the climate of East…
concepts.example: Chemical weathering changes the minerals in the rock into new substances, usually by reacting with water and gases in the air. Rain water is very slightly acidic, and over long times it can slowly dissolve certain…

explorations.title: Building Sedimentary Layers in a Jar
explorations.context: To model how sediment carried by a river settles into layers, just as it does on the bed of the Jubba or Shabelle river, and to see how sedimentary rock begins.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write: "When I shake up the soil and water and let it settle, I predict that the ... pieces will sink to the bottom first and the ... pieces will settle last on top."
explorations.answer: Which pieces settled first - the large heavy ones or the small light ones? Why? How do the layers in your jar look like the strata (layers) you might see in a cliff? In a real river, where would the heaviest gravel be dropped - where the water flows fast or where it slows down? If these layers were…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how sediment carried by a river settles into layers, just as it does on the bed of the Jubba or Shabelle river, and to see how sedimentary rock begins.

explorations.title: Weathering Rock with Warm and Cold Water
explorations.context: To model how the daily heating and cooling of the dry season breaks rock apart, using chalk or soft rock and warm and cold water.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think that heating the chalk and then cooling it quickly will make it ... (crack / stay the same), because ..."
explorations.answer: Did the repeatedly heated and cooled piece change more than the dry control piece? How? Why is it important to keep a control piece that is not heated and cooled? How is this experiment like a rock in the dry season, heated by the sun each day and cooled each night? Is this an example of weathering…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how the daily heating and cooling of the dry season breaks rock apart, using chalk or soft rock and warm and cold water.

explorations.title: The Scratch-Hardness Test
explorations.context: To compare the hardness of different rocks and minerals by testing which ones can scratch which, just as geologists do with the hardness scale.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think the hardest object I test will be ... and the softest will be ... ."
explorations.answer: Which object was the hardest, and how did you know? Which was the softest? What could scratch it? Why can a harder mineral scratch a softer one but not the reverse? Why is hardness a useful property when choosing a rock to build with or to make a tool?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To compare the hardness of different rocks and minerals by testing which ones can scratch which, just as geologists do with the hardness scale.

explorations.title: What Is Soil Made Of? (The Soil-Settling Test)
explorations.context: To find out what soil is made of by letting it settle in water, revealing its layers of sand, silt, clay and floating humus.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think garden soil is made of ... and ... . When I settle it in water I think I will see ... layers."
explorations.answer: What settled at the bottom, and what settled near the top? Why did they separate this way? What do you think the bits floating on the surface are made of? Which part of the soil - sand, clay or humus - do you think helps plants grow the most, and why? How does this show that soil is a mixture and…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out what soil is made of by letting it settle in water, revealing its layers of sand, silt, clay and floating humus.

methods.title: Building Sedimentary Layers in a Jar
methods.example: To model how sediment carried by a river settles into layers, just as it does on the bed of the Jubba or Shabelle river, and to see how sedimentary rock begins.
steps: Put the mixed soil into the jar until it is about one-third full.
Add water until the jar is about three-quarters full, leaving space at the top.
Screw the lid on tightly and shake the jar hard for about thirty seconds, so everything is mixed and the water looks muddy.
Stand the jar on a table where it will not be disturbed and start watching.
Observe after 1 minute, 10 minutes, 1 hour and again the next day. Notice the order in which the pieces settle.

methods.title: Weathering Rock with Warm and Cold Water
methods.example: To model how the daily heating and cooling of the dry season breaks rock apart, using chalk or soft rock and warm and cold water.
steps: Look closely at each piece of chalk and note whether it is smooth or already cracked.
Place a piece into the warm water for one minute so it heats up.
Using a spoon, move it straight into the cold water for one minute so it cools quickly.
Repeat this warm-then-cold cycle five or six times for the same piece.
Keep a second piece as a 'control' that stays dry and is not heated or cooled.

methods.title: The Scratch-Hardness Test
methods.example: To compare the hardness of different rocks and minerals by testing which ones can scratch which, just as geologists do with the hardness scale.
steps: Line up your objects. You will test which object can scratch which.
Start soft: try to scratch each stone with your fingernail. Note which ones get a mark.
Next try scratching each stone with a coin, then with an iron nail.
Then try using each stone to scratch the back of a ceramic tile or a piece of glass (with adult help).
Remember the rule: a HARDER object scratches a SOFTER one, but not the other way round.

methods.title: What Is Soil Made Of? (The Soil-Settling Test)
methods.example: To find out what soil is made of by letting it settle in water, revealing its layers of sand, silt, clay and floating humus.
steps: Put the soil into the jar until it is about one-third full.
Fill the jar most of the way with water, leaving a gap at the top.
Screw on the lid and shake hard for about a minute until the soil is fully mixed with the water.
Set the jar down where it will not be disturbed.
Watch it settle. The heavy sand drops first, then the finer silt, then the very fine clay - and bits of humus (rotted plants) often float on the top.

workedExamples.title: s - Identifying and Reasoning About Rocks
workedExamples.prompt: Follow the reasoning in each example. In every one, notice how we look for clues in the rock and use them to work out an answer, just as a geologist does.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1 - Naming a rock type from clues Question: Amina finds a pale, layered rock in a cliff. When she looks closely she can see the shapes of tiny shells in it, arranged in flat bands. What type of rock is it, and how do you know? Step 1 - Look for clues. It is in layers (strata), and it contains the shells of living things. Step 2 - Match the clues to a rock family. Layers and fossils are the fingerprints of sedimentary rock, which forms from settled sediment and the remains of living things.

workedExamples.title: 1 - Naming a rock type from clues
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Amina finds a pale, layered rock in a cliff. When she looks closely she can see the shapes of tiny shells in it, arranged in flat bands. What type of rock is it, and how do you know?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Look for clues. It is in layers (strata), and it contains the shells of living things. Step 2 - Match the clues to a rock family. Layers and fossils are the fingerprints of sedimentary rock, which forms from settled sediment and the remains of living things. Answer: It is a sedimentary rock (a limestone). We know because sedimentary rock is the only family that forms gently in layers and preserves fossils. Worked Example 2 - Reasoning about crystal size

workedExamples.title: 2 - Reasoning about crystal size
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Farah has two igneous rocks. Rock A has large crystals; Rock B has crystals too small to see. Which one cooled slowly, and which cooled quickly?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Recall the rule. Slow cooling gives crystals time to grow large; fast cooling gives small crystals. Step 2 - Apply it. Rock A (large crystals) must have cooled slowly, deep underground, like granite. Rock B (tiny crystals) must have cooled quickly, at the surface, like basalt from a lava flow. Answer: Rock A cooled slowly underground; Rock B cooled quickly at the surface. The crystal size is the clue. Worked Example 3 - Reading the order of layers

workedExamples.title: 3 - Reading the order of layers
workedExamples.prompt: Question: In a cliff, Halima sees three layers of sedimentary rock: sandstone at the bottom, mudstone in the middle, and limestone at the top. Which layer formed first?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Recall the rule. Each new layer settles on top of the older ones, so the oldest layer is at the bottom. Step 2 - Apply it. The bottom layer is the sandstone, so it was laid down first, before the mudstone and limestone above it. Answer: The sandstone at the bottom formed first. Reading strata from bottom to top reads them from oldest to youngest. Worked Example 4 - A hardness test

workedExamples.title: 4 - A hardness test
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Yusuf tests three minerals by trying to scratch them. Mineral X scratches the other two but nothing scratches it. Mineral Z is scratched by both of the others. Which mineral is hardest, and which is softest?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Recall the idea. A harder mineral can scratch a softer one, but a softer mineral cannot scratch a harder one. Step 2 - Apply it. Mineral X scratches everything and is scratched by nothing, so it is the hardest. Mineral Z is scratched by both others, so it is the softest. Answer: Mineral X is the hardest and Mineral Z is the softest. This scratch test is exactly how geologists compare hardness, using a scale that runs from soft talc up to hard diamond. Reading Comprehension - The Stone Houses of the Coast

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Rock formed from layers of pressed and cemented sediment is: (a) igneous (b) metamorphic (c) sedimentary (d) volcanic
workedExamples.solution: (c) sedimentary - it forms from pressed and cemented layers of sediment.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: The breaking down of rock where it stands is called: (a) erosion (b) deposition (c) weathering (d) cementation
workedExamples.solution: (c) weathering - weathering breaks rock where it stands.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The carrying away of broken rock pieces is called: (a) weathering (b) erosion (c) compaction (d) melting
workedExamples.solution: (b) erosion - erosion carries the broken pieces away.

practice.prompt: A natural solid usually made of a mixture of minerals is called a: (a) mineral (b) rock (c) fossil (d) crystal
practice.answer: (b) rock - a rock is a natural solid usually made of a mixture of minerals.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Rock formed when molten rock cools and hardens is called: (a) sedimentary (b) metamorphic (c) igneous (d) fossil rock
practice.answer: (c) igneous - it forms when molten rock cools and hardens.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Molten rock while it is still beneath the surface is called: (a) lava (b) magma (c) sediment (d) humus
practice.answer: (b) magma - molten rock below the surface is magma.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Molten rock that has flowed out of a volcano is called: (a) magma (b) basalt (c) lava (d) strata
practice.answer: (c) lava - molten rock that reaches the surface is lava.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An igneous rock with large crystals cooled: (a) quickly at the surface (b) slowly deep underground (c) in water (d) in the air
practice.answer: (b) slowly deep underground - slow cooling gives crystals time to grow large.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Rock formed from layers of pressed and cemented sediment is: (a) igneous (b) metamorphic (c) sedimentary (d) volcanic
practice.answer: (c) sedimentary - it forms from pressed and cemented layers of sediment.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The breaking down of rock where it stands is called: (a) erosion (b) deposition (c) weathering (d) cementation
practice.answer: (c) weathering - weathering breaks rock where it stands.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The carrying away of broken rock pieces is called: (a) weathering (b) erosion (c) compaction (d) melting
practice.answer: (b) erosion - erosion carries the broken pieces away.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Fossils are almost always found in which type of rock? (a) igneous (b) sedimentary (c) metamorphic (d) molten
practice.answer: (b) sedimentary - only sedimentary rock forms gently enough to preserve fossils.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Rock changed by great heat and pressure without melting is: (a) igneous (b) sedimentary (c) metamorphic (d) sediment
practice.answer: (c) metamorphic - it is changed by heat and pressure without melting.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Marble is formed when heat and pressure change: (a) sandstone (b) mudstone (c) limestone (d) granite
practice.answer: (c) limestone - heat and pressure change limestone into marble.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The never-ending process that changes one rock type into another is the: (a) water cycle (b) rock cycle (c) life cycle (d) food chain
practice.answer: (b) rock cycle - the endless process that changes one rock type into another.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Building Sedimentary Layers in a Jar
activities.materials: A clear jar or bottle with a tight lid.; A few handfuls of garden soil that contains a mix of sizes - some gravel or small stones, sand, and fine dust or clay.; Water to fill the jar about three-quarters full.; Optional: a spoonful of sand and a spoonful of fine flour or clay to add extra sizes.
steps: Put the mixed soil into the jar until it is about one-third full.
Add water until the jar is about three-quarters full, leaving space at the top.
Screw the lid on tightly and shake the jar hard for about thirty seconds, so everything is mixed and the water looks muddy.
Stand the jar on a table where it will not be disturbed and start watching.
Observe after 1 minute, 10 minutes, 1 hour and again the next day. Notice the order in which the pieces settle.

activities.title: Weathering Rock with Warm and Cold Water
activities.materials: Two or three pieces of blackboard chalk, or small pieces of soft, dry rock or a dry clay brick.; A cup of warm water (an adult prepares this) and a cup of cold water.; A spoon or tongs to move the pieces.; A magnifying glass if you have one, to see small cracks.
steps: Look closely at each piece of chalk and note whether it is smooth or already cracked.
Place a piece into the warm water for one minute so it heats up.
Using a spoon, move it straight into the cold water for one minute so it cools quickly.
Repeat this warm-then-cold cycle five or six times for the same piece.
Keep a second piece as a 'control' that stays dry and is not heated or cooled.

activities.title: The Scratch-Hardness Test
activities.materials: A few different stones or rocks collected from around your home.; A piece of chalk (soft), a coin, an iron nail or key, and a piece of glass or a ceramic tile (an unglazed back of a tile works well).; Your fingernail (a soft natural scratcher).; An adult to supervise, because some items are sharp.
steps: Line up your objects. You will test which object can scratch which.
Start soft: try to scratch each stone with your fingernail. Note which ones get a mark.
Next try scratching each stone with a coin, then with an iron nail.
Then try using each stone to scratch the back of a ceramic tile or a piece of glass (with adult help).
Remember the rule: a HARDER object scratches a SOFTER one, but not the other way round.

activities.title: What Is Soil Made Of? (The Soil-Settling Test)
activities.materials: A clear jar with a lid.; A cup of garden or farm soil.; Water to fill the jar most of the way.; A little patience - the fine clay can take a day or more to settle.
steps: Put the soil into the jar until it is about one-third full.
Fill the jar most of the way with water, leaving a gap at the top.
Screw on the lid and shake hard for about a minute until the soil is fully mixed with the water.
Set the jar down where it will not be disturbed.
Watch it settle. The heavy sand drops first, then the finer silt, then the very fine clay - and bits of humus (rotted plants) often float on the top.

terms: Rock
A natural solid, usually made of a mixture of minerals.
Mineral
A natural solid with a fixed make-up; the building block of rocks.
Igneous rock
Rock formed when molten rock (magma or lava) cools and hardens.
Sedimentary rock
Rock formed when layers of sediment are pressed and cemented together.
Metamorphic rock
Rock changed by great heat and pressure, without melting.
Magma
Molten rock beneath the Earth's surface.
Lava
Molten rock that has reached the surface.
Weathering
The breaking down of rock where it stands.
Erosion
The carrying away of broken rock pieces by water, wind, ice or gravity.
Sediment
Small loose pieces of weathered rock: gravel, sand, silt and mud.
Deposition
When moving water or wind slows and drops its sediment.
Compaction
The pressing together of sediment layers by the weight above.
commonMistakes: Thinking a rock and a mineral are the same.
Thinking fossils are found in any rock.
Thinking magma and lava are different materials.
The name is not a new substance.

realProblems.prompt: Builders in Lamu and Mombasa cut soft coral limestone into blocks. What type of rock is this, and how did it form from a coral reef?
realProblems.answer: 1. It is sedimentary rock (a coral limestone). It formed when a living coral reef of tiny sea creatures died and their hard skeletons piled up in layers on the sea floor; over long ages the layers were pressed and cemented into solid rock, which later rose above the sea and could be quarried.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. It is sedimentary rock (a coral limestone). It formed when a living coral reef of tiny sea creatures died and their hard skeletons piled up in layers on the sea floor; over long ages the layers were pressed and cemented into solid rock, which later rose above the sea and could be quarried.

realProblems.prompt: The Great Rift Valley has many volcanoes that have poured out dark basalt. What type of rock is basalt, and why does it have very small crystals?
realProblems.answer: 2. Basalt is an igneous rock. It has very small crystals because the lava cooled quickly at the surface, giving the crystals no time to grow large.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. Basalt is an igneous rock. It has very small crystals because the lava cooled quickly at the surface, giving the crystals no time to grow large.

realProblems.prompt: During the wet season the Jubba River runs brown and muddy, but in the dry season it runs clearer. Explain what the brown colour is and why there is more of it in the wet season.
realProblems.answer: 3. The brown colour is sediment - sand, silt and mud - carried in the water by erosion. There is more in the wet season because the river runs faster and fuller, so it can carry much more sediment; in the dry season the slower river carries and drops less.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. The brown colour is sediment - sand, silt and mud - carried in the water by erosion. There is more in the wet season because the river runs faster and fuller, so it can carry much more sediment; in the dry season the slower river carries and drops less.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near an old volcano has dark, rich soil, while a farmer in a dry area has thin, sandy soil. Explain why their soils are so different.
realProblems.answer: 4. The dark soil formed from weathered volcanic rock, which is rich in the minerals plants need, and it holds more humus, so it is fertile. The sandy soil comes from weathered rock with large grains and little humus, so it drains quickly, dries out and is less fertile.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. The dark soil formed from weathered volcanic rock, which is rich in the minerals plants need, and it holds more humus, so it is fertile. The sandy soil comes from weathered rock with large grains and little humus, so it drains quickly, dries out and is less fertile.

realProblems.prompt: Scientists searching for ancient fossils in the Rift Valley always dig in sedimentary rock, never in lava rock. Explain why.
realProblems.answer: 5. Because fossils only form and survive in sedimentary rock, which is laid down gently in layers. Lava (igneous) rock forms from molten rock so hot it would destroy any remains, so no fossils can be found there.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. Because fossils only form and survive in sedimentary rock, which is laid down gently in layers. Lava (igneous) rock forms from molten rock so hot it would destroy any remains, so no fossils can be found there.

realProblems.prompt: Describe the full journey of a single rock all the way around the rock cycle, starting as lava from a Rift Valley volcano and ending back as igneous rock. Name each type of rock and each process.
realProblems.answer: 1. Lava cools and hardens into igneous rock (e.g. basalt). Weathering and erosion break it into sediment and carry it away. Deposition drops the sediment in layers, which compaction and cementation harden into sedimentary rock (e.g. sandstone). Deep burial with heat and pressure changes it into…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.

Deposition drops the sediment in layers, which compaction and cementation harden into sedimentary rock (e.g. sandstone). Deep burial with heat and pressure changes it into…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a rock and a mineral, with one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. A mineral is a natural solid with a fixed make-up and is a single pure substance (for example, quartz or salt). A rock is a natural solid usually made of a mixture of minerals joined together (for example, granite, which contains quartz, feldspar and mica).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three main types of rock and say in one sentence how each one forms.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Igneous rock forms when molten rock (magma or lava) cools and hardens. Sedimentary rock forms when layers of sediment are pressed and cemented together. Metamorphic rock forms when an existing rock is changed by great heat and pressure without melting.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between magma and lava?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. They are the same molten rock. It is called magma while it is beneath the surface, and lava once it has flowed out onto the surface.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain how the speed of cooling affects the crystal size of an igneous rock.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Slow cooling deep underground gives crystals a long time to grow, so they are large (as in granite). Fast cooling at the surface gives crystals no time to grow, so they are small (as in basalt).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List, in order, the four steps that turn loose sediment into sedimentary rock.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. (i) Weathering breaks rock into pieces; (ii) erosion carries the pieces away; (iii) deposition drops them in layers; (iv) compaction and cementation press and glue the layers into solid rock.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between weathering and erosion.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Weathering is the breaking down of rock where it stands. Erosion is the carrying away of the broken pieces by water, wind, ice or gravity. Break first, then transport.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can explain the difference between a rock and a mineral, with everyday examples.
I can describe the three main types of rock - igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic - and how each one forms.
I can explain how weathering breaks rock down and how erosion carries the pieces away.
I can describe how loose pieces (sediment) are transported by rivers and settle into layers that harden into sedimentary rock.
I can explain how fossils form and why they are almost always found in sedimentary rock.
I can describe the rock cycle and explain how one type of rock can slowly turn into another.
I can explain how soil forms from weathered rock and why healthy soil matters for farming.
